**Key facts:**
- A perfect NCAA March Madness bracket requires correctly predicting all 63 games in the tournament.
- The theoretical probability of a perfect bracket by random coin flips is 1 in 2^63 ≈ 1 in 9.2 quintillion.
- Even with basketball knowledge, estimates from mathematicians (e.g., DePaul professor Jay Bergen) put the odds at roughly 1 in 9.2 billion to 1 in 120 billion.
- No one has ever achieved a perfect bracket in the history of tracked bracket pools (ESPN, Yahoo, CBS, NCAA.com, etc.).
- The longest known streak of correct picks to start a tournament was 49 games (in 2019).
- Millions of brackets are filled out each year (estimated 60-100 million), so even with informed picks, the probability remains astronomically low.

**Calculation:**
- Even at the most generous estimate of 1 in 9.2 billion, with ~100 million brackets submitted, the probability of at least one perfect bracket in a given year is roughly 100,000,000 / 9,200,000,000 ≈ 1.1%.
- But the market asks about "a perfect bracket" which likely means anyone achieving it. The resolution date of April 7, 2026 corresponds to the end of the 2026 NCAA tournament.
- The market price of $0.01 (1%) seems roughly in line with the most optimistic estimates, though I think even that is generous.

Given that no one has ever done this, and the mathematical odds are extraordinarily against it even with informed picking across all submitted brackets, the true probability is well below 1%. I'd estimate it at approximately 0.1% to 0.5% at most.
